text.title: Decimal-as-string with regex pattern
text.summary: Use pattern-constrained strings for money and exact decimals so the decoder cannot drift on digit count or precision.

section.title: The rule
section.p: Any field whose exact textual form matters — money, big integers, identifiers, version strings — is declared as str with a regex pattern, not as a numeric JSON type. Parse to the appropriate exact type (Decimal, int, datetime) only after the model has produced a known-good string.

rule.statement: For currency, exact decimals, big integers (greater than 2^53), and structured identifiers, declare the schema field as a string with a regex pattern that pins the exact surface form, then parse to Decimal/int/datetime on the receiving side.
rule.rationale: JSON number types do not pin precision, and float deserialization can introduce binary-rounding drift. Regex-constrained strings are masked at decode time on strict-mode or grammar backends — the model literally cannot emit "19.989999" when the pattern demands two decimal places.

example.code:
from decimal import Decimal from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
class LineItem(BaseModel): sku: str price_usd: str = Field( pattern=r"^\d+\.\d{2}$", description="USD price as decimal string e.g. '19.99'. Two decimal places required.", ) quantity: int # OK as int — small counts, no precision issue
# Receiving side: parse exactly, no float drift. item = LineItem(sku="A1", price_usd="19.99", quantity=3) total: Decimal = Decimal(item.price_usd) * item.quantity # Decimal('59.97')
example.note: The pattern forbids "19", "19.9", "19.999". Decimal arithmetic on the parsed string is exact. No float drift anywhere in the path.

example.code:
class LineItem(BaseModel): price_usd: float
# Model emits 19.99; pydantic parses it as 19.989999999999998 on some platforms. total = item.price_usd * 3 # 59.96999999999999 — wrong for a billing system.
example.note: Float storage corrupts the exact value before any business logic runs. Sums diverge from cents-accurate totals.

antipattern.description: Modeling currency as a JSON number (float) and "rounding to two decimals at the end".
antipattern.reason: Compounding drift across many line items pushes totals off by one or more cents, which fails reconciliation. The Base-10 digit-encoding paper shows LLMs emit numbers as digit tokens anyway, so a string field is closer to the model's native representation while gaining exact arithmetic on the consumer side.

section.reference: Working invoice schema with pattern-constrained price and big-int identifier.
Promptfoo — LLM evaluation techniques for JSON outputs.
Language Models Encode Numbers Using Digit Representations in Base 10 (arXiv 2410.11781).

see-also.link: Reference regex patterns for common decimal/identifier shapes.
